🚗 Vehicle Price Prediction — Model Training Report

Dataset: Vehicle Sales Data (Kaggle)  |  Task: Regression — predict sellingprice  |  Train/Test split: 80/20  |  Total samples: ~49,500

Dataset Summary

After preprocessing: 49,501 rows × 212 features. Features include scaled numerical columns (year, odometer, condition, age, age×odometer), One-Hot encoded categoricals (make, body, transmission, state, color, interior), and Hash-encoded high-cardinality features (model: 32 components, trim: 16 components).

1. Ridge Regression

Model Type

Linear regression with L2 regularization. Adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared coefficients, helping with multicollinearity in high-dimensional OHE features.

Grid Search Parameters

ParameterValues searchedBest value
alpha (regularization strength)[0.1, 1.0, 10.0, 100.0]1.0

CV folds: 3  |  Scoring: neg_root_mean_squared_error

Metrics

$4,058
RMSE
$2,952
MAE
0.7729
R²

Plots

The residuals plot shows a fan-shaped (heteroscedastic) pattern — variance increases with price. This is expected for linear regression on price data. The QQ plot reveals consistent under-prediction for very expensive vehicles.

2. Random Forest Regressor

Model Type

Ensemble of decision trees trained on random feature subsets (bagging). Naturally handles non-linearity, feature interactions, and provides feature importances.

Grid Search Parameters

ParameterValues searchedBest value
n_estimators[50, 100]100
max_depth[10, 20]20
criterion['squared_error', 'absolute_error']squared_error

CV folds: 3  |  Scoring: neg_root_mean_squared_error

Metrics

$3,138
RMSE
$2,116
MAE
0.8642
R²

Plots

Feature Importances

Year, odometer, and condition dominate feature importances, confirming the EDA findings. The hash-encoded model/trim features also contribute substantially.

3. Gradient Boosting Regressor

Model Type

Sequential ensemble: each tree corrects residuals of the previous. More accurate than Random Forest on many tasks, but more prone to overfitting and slower to train.

Grid Search Parameters

ParameterValues searchedBest value
n_estimators[50, 100]100
max_depth[3, 5]5
learning_rate[0.05, 0.1]0.1

CV folds: 3  |  Scoring: neg_root_mean_squared_error

Metrics

$3,248
RMSE
$2,251
MAE
0.8545
R²

Plots

4. K-Nearest Neighbors Regressor

Model Type

Instance-based learning: prediction is a weighted average of k nearest neighbours in feature space. Effective when similar cars have similar prices (local market patterns). Requires well-scaled features.

Grid Search Parameters

ParameterValues searchedBest value
n_neighbors[10, 30, 50]10
weights['uniform', 'distance']distance
metric['cosine']cosine

Search method: Manual iteration (memory-efficient grid search)

Metrics

$3,091
RMSE
$2,012
MAE
0.8682
R²

Plots

Models Comparison

ModelRMSE ($)MAE ($)R²Rank
KNN (n=10, distance, cosine)3,0912,0120.8682🥇 1
Random Forest (100 trees, depth=20)3,1382,1160.8642🥈 2
Gradient Boosting (lr=0.1, depth=5)3,2482,2510.8545🥉 3
Ridge Regression (alpha=1.0)4,0582,9520.77294

Best Model

Selected model: K-Nearest Neighbors Regressor (n_neighbors=10, weights='distance', metric='cosine')

Primary metric used: R² (coefficient of determination)

Justification:

  • KNN achieves the highest R² = 0.8682, meaning it explains 86.82% of variance in car prices — the highest among all models.
  • KNN also achieves the lowest RMSE ($3,091) and lowest MAE ($2,012), meaning its predictions are closest to actual prices on average.
  • The cosine distance metric works well here because the feature vectors (after OHE and hash encoding) are high-dimensional and sparse — cosine similarity captures directional similarity rather than raw magnitude distance.
  • Distance-weighted voting (closer neighbors contribute more) further improves accuracy over uniform weighting.
  • R² was chosen as the primary metric because it is scale-independent and directly interpretable: R²=0.87 means our model explains 87% of price variance, which is practically useful for pricing decisions.

Note: Random Forest (R²=0.8642) is a close second and would be preferred for production deployment due to faster inference and explainability via feature importances. KNN scales poorly with dataset size at inference time.

Report generated for E2E ML Project · 2026
